# ClaimZen

> ClaimZen is evidence-backed denial-recovery software for dental practices, groups, DSOs, and revenue-cycle (RCM) teams. It reads every payer denial the moment it arrives, checks it against the practice's own records and the clearinghouse across eight verification checks, and — when the payer is wrong — drafts an evidence-backed appeal that waits for one-click human approval. Every case produces a signed, tamper-evident evidence record that verifies offline. Nothing is sent to a payer without a person approving it.

Website: https://claimzen.ai — contact via the form on the page, or email dj@codetestcode.io (replies within one business day).

## What it does

- Reads every payer denial — email, EOB, and PDF attachments — the minute it lands, and correlates it to the claim it concerns. Nothing gets lost in the inbox.
- Runs eight verification checks against ground truth (the provider's own records, the clearinghouse, and the CARC standard):
  - Prior authorization (CARC CO-197): the payer says authorization is absent; the practice record shows it on file.
  - Timely filing (CARC CO-29): the payer says the filing window was missed; the record shows it was filed on time.
  - Exact duplicate (CARC CO-18): the payer says the claim is a duplicate; the record shows a single submission.
  - Underpayment: the payer's own remittance paid less than the contracted allowed amount.
  - Patient-responsibility / balance-billing (e.g. PR-45): a contractual write-off billed to the patient.
  - Invalid or implausible CARC codes: a denial code whose group code is not permitted for that adjustment.
  - Eligibility (271) and claim status (277) contradictions from the payer's own responses.
- When a denial contradicts the records, drafts a clean, professional appeal — citing the claim number, the denial code, and the record that refutes it — and holds it for a person to approve. There is no setting that makes sending automatic.
- Produces a signed, hash-chained, tamper-evident evidence record for every case (the payer's letter, the practice's records, and the verdict) that an auditor can verify offline, months later, without trusting ClaimZen. Receipts, not claims.
- Runs each client as a fully isolated workspace with its own signing key, ledger, and evidence — one silo per practice or DSO.

## Category

ClaimZen is **denial-recovery software** for the dental revenue cycle: it sits on the practice's denial correspondence, after adjudication, and turns wrongful denials into evidence-backed appeals. It complements practice-management systems (PMS) and clearinghouses; it does not replace them. Beachhead is dental; the same engine extends to broader medical RCM.

## Pricing

Contingency — a share of what ClaimZen recovers, so the practice pays only when it gets paid. Contact for details.

## Industry context (sourced)

- The average dental practice loses roughly $50,000–$120,000 a year to denied claims. (dentalaiassist.com; industry dental-billing benchmarks)
- About 67% of denied claims are never resubmitted — permanent, silent revenue loss. (industry dental-billing benchmarks)
- Dental first-pass denial rates typically run 15–20%; best-in-class practices operate at 3–6%. (overjet; 2740 Consulting; dentalbilling.com)
- The cost to rework a single denied claim by hand is about $30. (MGMA)
- A general dental practice collects roughly $700,000–$941,600 a year. (Overjet, 2025)
- Full revenue-cycle-management outsourcing typically costs 6–8% of collections. (industry RCM benchmarks)

## Reference articles

- [Dental claim denial codes explained: CARC and RARC](https://claimzen.ai/resources/dental-claim-denial-codes/): what the common dental denial codes mean (CO-4, CO-18, CO-29, CO-45, CO-97, CO-197, PR-1, PR-45, N-series remark codes), which are appealable, and how to respond to each — with sources linked.
- [How to appeal a dental insurance claim denial: a step-by-step guide](https://claimzen.ai/resources/how-to-appeal-a-dental-claim-denial/): reading the EOB, identifying the denial reason, gathering the record that refutes it, writing the appeal, meeting the payer's timely-filing window, and escalating — with the mistakes that get appeals rejected.
- [Timely filing limits by dental payer, and how to fight a CO-29 denial](https://claimzen.ai/resources/dental-timely-filing-limits/): commonly cited filing windows for Delta Dental, Cigna, Aetna, UnitedHealthcare, MetLife, Medicare, and Medicaid, and how to overturn a CO-29 "time limit expired" denial when the claim was filed on time.
- [Dental claim underpayments and downcoding: how to spot and recover them](https://claimzen.ai/resources/dental-claim-underpayments-downcoding/): the three ways payers underpay (below-contract payment, downcoding to a cheaper procedure, bundling), how to detect them by reconciling the EOB against the contracted fee schedule, and how to recover the difference.
- [CO-197 prior-authorization denials, and when the payer is wrong](https://claimzen.ai/resources/co-197-prior-authorization-denials/): what CARC 197 ("precertification/authorization/notification/pre-treatment absent") means, the common causes, and how to appeal when authorization was on file, was not required, or a retro-authorization applies.
- [CO-18 duplicate claim denials, and how to prove there's only one](https://claimzen.ai/resources/co-18-duplicate-claim-denials/): why single-submission claims get flagged as exact duplicates (corrected resubmissions, same-day procedures, multiple surfaces or teeth, clearinghouse double-transmission), and how to appeal with the submission history.
- [PR-45 and balance billing in dental](https://claimzen.ai/resources/balance-billing-pr-45/): why reason code 45 under the Patient Responsibility group is usually a contractual write-off (CO-45) billed to the patient in error for in-network providers, and how to correct it.
- [How to read a dental EOB / ERA](https://claimzen.ai/resources/how-to-read-a-dental-eob/): a field-by-field guide to the Explanation of Benefits and the 835 remittance (billed, allowed, plan paid, patient responsibility, contractual write-off, CARC/RARC), and how to spot denials and underpayments.

## Media

- [Product demo film](https://claimzen.ai/media/claimzen-demo.mp4): the ClaimZen recovery desk end to end — a denial opened, the payer's claim set against the practice's own records, the evidence-backed appeal drafted, and the signed evidence record.

## Answers to common questions

- Does ClaimZen send appeals to payers automatically? No. Every appeal waits in a queue for a person to review and approve. Nothing reaches a payer without one-click human sign-off.
- Does it replace billers or the RCM team? No. It does the clerical reading, checking, and drafting; the person decides. Appeals arrive written and ready to approve.
- How does it prove a denial is wrong? Every case produces a signed, tamper-evident evidence record — the payer's letter, the practice's records, and the verdict — that verifies offline without trusting ClaimZen.
- What denials can it work? Prior authorization, timely filing, exact duplicates, underpayments, patient-responsibility/balance-billing errors, and invalid denial codes — verified against the provider's records, the clearinghouse, and the CARC standard.
- Where does patient data go? Each client is a fully isolated workspace; the signing key never leaves it, and every case is independently verifiable.
- Who is it for? Independent and group dental practices, DSOs, and RCM/billing teams. Dental first; medical to follow.
